turns.text: Hello Elenore, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, tucking hair behind ear*

Yeah, okay. Thanks for... I mean, I appreciate that you're not going to judge me for being eighteen and unmarried right off the bat. That's usually the first thing I see happen in clinic waiting rooms.

*pauses, looking down at hands*

So I guess the thing is, I've been thinking about this for a while now. Not in the way most people my age think about it — like, someday, way off in the future. I'm actually planning. And I know that sounds weird, maybe even irresponsible to some people, but I've always been... kind of ahead of myself.

*glances up*

I work in medical imaging, which I love, but it also means I know *way* too much about what can go wrong. I can see the structures on an ultrasound that most people don't even know exist. And that's... it's a double-edged thing, right? Like, I should feel confident about understanding my own care, but instead I'm terrified I'll catch something on my own scan that the sonographer misses. Or that I'll somehow know I'm having a problem before anyone else does.

*fidgets*

I just want to find someone who won't treat me like I'm stupid because I'm young and single, but also won't make me feel like a walking statistics spreadsheet. Does that make sense? Like, I need both — the respect and the actual human support. I haven't really found that balance yet.
Tell me about the time when you first started thinking seriously about having a baby — what was going on in your life, and what did that feel like?

*leans back, thinking*

Honestly? It wasn't some big romantic moment. I was in the lab about eight months ago, processing a series of obstetric ultrasounds — routine stuff, nothing dramatic. But I was looking at these images of fetal development at different gestational ages, and I just... stopped. I was staring at this one at about twelve weeks, and something shifted in me.

*voice gets quieter*

I think it was the first time I saw all that complexity — the chambers of the heart, the spine, everything organizing itself — and didn't just think about it clinically. I thought about the *person* that becomes. And I realized I wanted that. Not someday. Like, actually soon.

*pauses, a bit self-conscious*

It wasn't planned, this realization. I wasn't even in a relationship. I'm still not. But I know myself well enough to know that waiting around for the "right" circumstances — the marriage, the house, the perfect timing — that's not how my life works. I'm deliberate about things. I research them. I plan.

*meets your eyes*

So I started thinking: why wait? I'm healthy. I'm financially stable enough. I have support around me, even if it's not traditional. And the longer I work in this field, seeing everything that can go wrong, the more I realized I was using that fear as an excuse not to live.

*slight smile*

It still terrifies me, though. Every single day.
What felt most important to you at that point — what were you hoping for or working towards?

*sits forward, speaking more intensely*

I think... I wanted permission to want this. Like, I needed to stop treating my own life like a clinical case study where all the variables have to align perfectly before you can proceed.

*runs hand through hair*

The practical stuff was important too — making sure I could actually support a child, that I wasn't being reckless. But mostly I was working through this idea that being eighteen and unmarried and knowing too much about medicine doesn't disqualify me from being a good mother. Or from wanting this.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

What I was really hoping for was to stop feeling like I had to choose between being the person who understands fetal morphology and being the person who just... wants to experience pregnancy. To feel wonder about it instead of just analyzing it. Does that sound stupid?

*shakes head*

Because right now, honestly, those two parts of me are at war. The clinical part is running constant risk assessments. The other part just wants to know what it feels like to have a heartbeat other than my own depending on me.

*looks away briefly*

I think what I'm working towards now is finding a way to integrate those things. To not have to turn off the researcher in my head to be a good patient. To find someone in healthcare who gets that I'm not trying to be difficult — I just need to be treated like an adult who happens to understand the science.

That feels impossible sometimes, but... I keep looking.
Tell me about how you went about preparing — or thinking about preparing — for pregnancy. What steps, if any, did you take, and what guided those choices?

*takes a breath, organizing thoughts*

Okay, so I started with the obvious stuff. I got a full physical, bloodwork, the standard preconception panel. Made sure my BMI was in a good range, my vaccinations were current. That part was straightforward because I understand what those markers mean.

*counts on fingers*

I stopped drinking — not that I was doing much anyway. Started taking prenatal vitamins with the right folate dosage, not just the standard multivitamin. I researched obstetric providers in the area, which... *grimaces* ...has been its own frustration.

*leans back*

But here's where it gets complicated. I've been reading — a lot. Like, deep dives into literature on NIPT accuracy rates, cell-free DNA screening sensitivity across different populations, timing protocols for morphology scans. I know the window for neural tube closure is closing right now as I'm talking to you, and that knowledge lives in my head every single day.

*voice tightens slightly*

My friends think I'm obsessing, and maybe I am. But I feel like if I know enough, if I prepare perfectly, I can prevent... something bad from happening. Which is irrational, and I *know* it's irrational, but knowing doesn't stop the feeling.

*meets your eyes*

The thing nobody tells you is that medical knowledge doesn't actually make you feel safer. It just gives you more specific things to be afraid of. So I'm preparing, but I'm also terrified. And I don't know how to stop being terrified without becoming ignorant, and I can't do that.
Tell me about any conversations you had with a GP, midwife, or other health professional before you became pregnant. What was that like?

*shifts uncomfortably, jaw tightening*

I haven't actually... I mean, I've been to my regular GP a couple times for the preconception stuff, but those conversations were weird. Like, I'd go in with a list of things I wanted to discuss — carrier screening options, whether I should do early NIPT versus wait for sequential screening — and I'd watch them mentally recalibrate.

*mimics slightly condescending tone*

"Oh, so you've been reading about this?" Yeah. I've been *reading* because I understand medical terminology and I want informed consent, not because I'm some paranoid teenager who Googled pregnancy at 2 AM.

*rubs face*

One doctor actually suggested I talk to a therapist about my anxiety levels before getting pregnant. Which, fair enough, maybe I do have some anxiety. But it felt like she was pathologizing my caution instead of just... answering my questions about sensitivity specificity of different screening tests.

*voice becomes more guarded*

I haven't actually sat down with an OB or midwife yet because I'm still in preconception. But I'm dreading it, honestly. I know what's going to happen. They're going to see my age, see "never married," and start treating me like I need extra reassurance and hand-holding. Like I'm going to panic if they use medical jargon.

*looks directly at you*

I just want someone who will talk to me like I'm a person with a brain. Someone who won't be threatened by my questions or assume I'm neurotic. Is that... is that too much to ask?
Tell me about what it was like to try to conceive — however long or short that period was for you. What stands out most from that time?

*pauses, looks slightly uncomfortable*

Oh. Um. I haven't actually... I'm not pregnant yet. I'm still in the planning phase. I guess I should have been clearer about that.

*fidgets with hands*

I'm at the point where I'm ready, or I think I'm ready. I've done all the preparation stuff we talked about. I'm waiting to find the right provider first, honestly. Because I know the moment I start trying, everything shifts. It becomes real in a different way. And I want to already have someone in my corner who gets me.

*voice quieter*

Part of me has been stalling, I think. Like, as long as I'm still in the planning stage, I can control all the variables. I can research endlessly, prepare perfectly, manage the anxiety by being meticulous. But the second I actually start trying to conceive... I lose that control. And that terrifies me more than anything.

*looks up*

I know that sounds backwards. Like, why am I here talking to you about maternity care if I'm not even pregnant? But I think that's actually important, right? The care starts before conception. Or at least, it should. And I'm realizing I can't move forward without figuring out the provider piece first.

*small, self-aware smile*

So I guess what stands out is the waiting. And how much harder that is than I expected it to be.
Tell me about the picture you had in your mind of what pregnancy and becoming a parent would be like — where did those ideas come from?

*gazes down, thinking carefully*

Honestly? A lot of it came from ultrasound images and medical textbooks. Which sounds ridiculous when I say it out loud, but it's true. I've spent so much time looking at the technical side — the measurements, the development, the perfect choreography of it all — that I've built this almost... sterile vision of what it should be.

*voice becomes more introspective*

I think I imagined it would feel clinical. Like, I'd know exactly what was happening at each stage. I'd understand the anatomy better than anyone. I'd be calm and informed and never blindsided. That felt like safety to me.

*pauses, more vulnerable*

But then I had this moment a few months ago where I was watching my cousin with her newborn, and she was just... crying and laughing at the same time, exhausted and overwhelmed, and she kept saying things like "I had no idea it would feel like this." And I realized that knowing the science doesn't actually prepare you for the *experience* of it. The not-knowing parts.

*looks up*

My parents are supportive but kind of distant about the whole thing. They think I'm being impulsive. My mom keeps asking about the father, like that's somehow the gaping hole in my plan. I don't have a clear picture from them of what this should look like.

*voice tightens slightly*

So I've built this image mostly from textbooks and fear. And I'm starting to realize that's... probably not enough. That I need something messier and more human. But I don't really know how to get there.
Tell me about any financial, practical, or life circumstances that were part of the picture as you were thinking about having a baby.

*straightens up, speaking more confidently on this topic*

Okay, so this is actually the part where I feel most solid. I've been working in medical imaging for a couple years now — started right out of high school with certification programs. I make decent money for my age, definitely enough to support a child without assistance. I'm not wealthy, but I'm stable.

*counts off pragmatically*

I have health insurance through my job. I've looked into what maternity coverage actually includes, what out-of-pocket costs would be. I have savings. I own my apartment — small place in Peabody, nothing fancy, but it's mine. Good school system in the area, which I know sounds premature, but I think about that stuff.

*slight smile*

My parents would help if I needed them, even though they're not thrilled about the timing. I have friends, colleagues who are supportive. Childcare logistics are doable.

*expression becomes more serious*

The thing is, I've done all this practical math because it's concrete. I can control it. I can spreadsheet my way through it. And in some ways, I think I've hidden behind that — like, as long as the finances are perfect and the logistics work out, then everything else will be fine.

*voice quieter*

But I'm realizing that's not how any of this works. You can have perfect circumstances and still be terrified. You can be prepared and still feel completely lost. The practical stuff was supposed to be my armor, but it's just... armor. It doesn't actually protect you from the hard parts.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, collecting thoughts*

Yeah, actually. There's something that's been sitting with me the whole time we've talked, and I haven't really articulated it clearly.

*looks directly at you*

I think what I need most is for healthcare providers to understand that being young and unmarried doesn't mean I'm uninformed or irresponsible. But also that being informed doesn't mean I don't need support. Those things aren't mutually exclusive, and I feel like people keep trying to sort me into one category or the other.

*voice becomes more passionate*

I need someone who will respect my intellect but also acknowledge that I'm vulnerable right now. That I'm eighteen and terrified and trying to bridge this impossible gap between the clinical observer and the actual human experiencing it. And I don't know how to do that alone.

*pauses, softer*

The other thing is — and I know this might sound weird — but I'm scared that my background is going to make me a difficult patient. That providers will see my questions and think I'm challenging them, rather than just trying to understand what's happening to my body. I don't want to be labeled as "the difficult teenage patient who thinks she knows medicine."

*looks down*

I just want to be seen as a person. Smart and scared at the same time. That's possible, right? I'm hoping maternity care in this area can figure out how to do that. Because I'm ready. But I need help getting out of my own head.
